Epilepsy

Reason for letter: Guidance on using the NHS App for appointments and messages

Summary: Amelia Carter is the primary caregiver for young children and reports that night-time sleep can be interrupted. To support flexible access to care, we agreed to utilise the NHS App for routine communication and appointment management.

Guidance provided:
- Appointments: Future neurology reviews will be offered as telephone or video where appropriate. Appointment dates, times, and links will be sent via the NHS App. Please enable notifications and check the App weekly.
- Messaging: Non-urgent queries (e.g. medication timing, prescription requests, monitoring plans) should be sent using the NHS App messaging function. We aim to respond within 3–5 working days. For urgent clinical concerns, contact 111 or emergency services as appropriate.
- Documents: Clinic letters, care plans, and laboratory requests will be viewable in the App under ‘Messages’ and ‘Documents’. Patients can upload home monitoring notes as attachments prior to appointments if helpful.
- Prescriptions: Repeat prescriptions can be requested via the NHS App; choose the nominated pharmacy for collection or delivery. Allow 3 working days.
- Reminders: We recommended enabling calendar sync within the App to help manage appointments around childcare. Evening telephone slots can be requested via the App message if needed.

Next steps: A follow-up telephone review in 4 months will be arranged via the NHS App. Please contact us through the App if an earlier discussion is required.

Yours sincerely

metadata.initial_evidence:
Dear Dr Kate
Wendy Brown, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: K482715 NHS No. 6592841037
Flat 7 Brookside Avenue, Newcastle-under-Lyme, ST5 2QD

Diagnoses/summary: Seizure disorder of uncertain classification; post-partum within six months; sleep disruption related to infant care

MRI: none on record in our archive
EEG: routine EEG 2019 reportedly normal (patient account, external provider); formal report not available in our system

Previous medication: levetiracetam in past pregnancy (self-stopped post-delivery due to perceived mood effects), clobazam PRN trial during late pregnancy; no clear benefit documented

Not taken historically: lamotrigine, topiramate, zonisamide, lacosamide, valproate, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, brivaracetam, perampanel

Relevant medical history: uncomplicated vaginal delivery five months ago; breastfeeding; iron deficiency during third trimester treated with oral iron; no known cardiac disease

I reviewed Wendy Brown in clinic today, attended with her partner and infant. She reports that since delivery her night-time sleep has been fragmented, averaging 3–4 hours in blocks. She has implemented a seizure diary since mid-August. Since that time, she notes no witnessed events and no episodes suggestive of loss of awareness; in her words, she has had no seizures to her knowledge. She does describe occasional brief "head-fog" episodes when markedly sleep deprived, without motor features, injury, or post-event confusion. There have been no emergency presentations, no tongue biting, and no urinary incontinence. She is currently not driving and is aware of DVLA requirements; we reiterated standard safety advice (bathroom precautions, heights, and swimming supervision) today.

Current medication:
- Folic acid 5 mg daily (self-continued post-partum)
- Ferrous fumarate 210 mg daily (finishing remaining course)
- Vitamin D 1000 IU daily

Examination today was unremarkable; blood pressure 108/68, pulse 74 regular. Neurological examination normal. There is no clinical evidence of mood disorder; she reports some tiredness in the context of caring for her infant, with adequate social support.

Plan:
- Given her account of no current events since August and the post-partum sleep fragmentation, we agreed to continue observation without initiating anti-seizure medication today. We discussed potential triggers including broken sleep and the importance of structured rest where possible. She plans to trial alternating-night support with her partner to consolidate at least one longer sleep period.
- Safety: reinforced seizure first-aid education and infant-handling precautions (e.g. sitting for feeds when very fatigued). She will avoid solo bathing and will use a shower seat.
- Investigations: arrange routine EEG locally for baseline. If feasible with childcare, consider sleep-deprived EEG; we will decide after initial result. No immediate MRI planned unless events recur.
- DVLA: advised to continue to follow current guidance based on her history; she understands to inform DVLA and insurance as applicable if events recur or if there is diagnostic clarification.
- Follow-up: nurse specialist telephone review in 6–8 weeks to revisit diary entries and sleep pattern. Consultant review in clinic in 3–4 months or sooner if any events occur. She will contact us urgently if she has any episode with impaired awareness, injury, or if witnesses note concerning features.

I appreciate your ongoing support, particularly regarding sleep hygiene strategies and general post-partum wellbeing. Please let us know if there are concerns regarding mood or if you receive reports of possible events.

Yours sincerely
